evidence_quotes.quote: I would like for the Digital Fairness Act to also cover the end of life of digital goods like video games.
As I said before, this requirement is not technical in nature, but rather a requirement imposed by the developers to either control access or collect tracking data.

paragraphs.text: I would like for the Digital Fairness Act to also cover the end of life of digital goods like video games. Currently there is no law that dictates for companies to tell consumers how long the digital good is meant to last. There are many instances when a consumer purchases a digital good such as a game and after a few short years, the developer or IP holder of the digital good cuts off access by shutting down vital infrastructure such as a server or online service that is required to use the good. Most often than not, these good don't technically require the presence of the remote infrastructure to continue functioning but they are forced by the developers of the digital good. There are many single player video games for instance, that require an internet connection to authenticate with some server in order to be played. These servers cost companies money to maintain so inevitably the servers are shut down at some point and the game or software can no longer be used, leaving the buyer with nothing, leaving them no choice other than breaking copyright law to restore access. In some cases this isn't even an option at all. There are many ways to address this problem. At the very least, the company should provide some figure of time that they are guaranteed to support the digital good. But it would be best if at the end of the life of the product, they would let the open source community create the necessary infrastructure or a patch to remove reliance on said infrastructure. As I said before, this requirement is not technical in nature, but rather a requirement imposed by the developers to either control access or collect tracking data. Lastly, I urge the creators of this act to strongly consider this area, as the majority of consumers of digital goods such as video games are children, which are most vulnerable to being taken advantage of and as it is now, they are not protected against such practices. Thank you!
